conflicts.description: The current sheet says Carmen leaves Lakeview in chapter 18, but the manuscript keeps her actively working there on day shift while a New Brunswick interview is still ahead.
conflicts.canonical_value: Carmen is still employed at Lakeview in chapter 18, on day shift and interviewing at a New Brunswick hospital.
conflicts.resolution_note: Chapter 18 repeats the pending-interview status and still shows Carmen doing a full professional handoff, so the old sheet's completed departure was overstated.

conflicts.description: The current sheet dates ring hoarding to mid-2023, but the manuscript ties the origin to pharmacy failures and Agnes's unheeded ciprofloxacin/ropinirole warning, with the active sales side framed as roughly 18 months old.
conflicts.canonical_value: The ring begins as self-managed medication hoarding after pharmacy failures and Agnes's ignored interaction warning; the active selling side is roughly 18 months old by chapter 5.
conflicts.resolution_note: Repeated on-page references support a rough 18-month operating window, and the context pack's early-2025 sales intent fits that better than the old sheet's mid-2023 date, so this reads as an early-sheet timing error.

conflicts.description: Room 110's resident identity drifts across chapters: Mr. Delvecchio in chapter 1, Mrs. Davila in chapter 2, Mrs. Okafor in chapter 4, and Mrs. Delacroix in chapter 5.
conflicts.canonical_value: The sheet keeps only the stable fact that Room 110 is the source of a constant moan or murmur on Wing A; no occupant name is canonized.
conflicts.resolution_note: The room's sound and function are stable, but the names conflict too often and never become causally central enough to justify picking one over the others.

conflicts.description: Agnes's roommate identity changes repeatedly: Mrs. Nowak in chapter 1, Dorothy in chapter 2, Doris Okonkwo in chapter 8, Margaret in chapter 9, and Mrs. Delacroix in chapter 17.
conflicts.canonical_value: Agnes shares 108A with a high-care roommate with advanced dementia or Alzheimer's; the sheet omits a fixed roommate identity.
conflicts.resolution_note: The shared-room fact is stable and continuity-relevant; the roommate name is not, so the sheet keeps only the non-contradictory portion.

conflicts.description: Chapter 6 says Marcus has been signing in weekly for fourteen months, while chapter 5 already frames the operation as 'eighteen months, give or take' and chapter 10 gives the ring eighteen months of gross averages.
conflicts.canonical_value: The sheet treats the active selling side as roughly 18 months established by the main action, not a hard 14-month figure.
conflicts.resolution_note: The repeated 18-month framing is more embedded in the book's operational math and aligns better with planned intent; the single 14-month visitor-log line is treated as manuscript drift.
